End-to-End Supply Chain Visibility: Optimize and Innovate

About the Author

Darin Brannan

Chief Executive Officer

Darin is a seasoned founder and CEO with over 25 years of experience building category-defining SaaS and infrastructure companies, leading them from early stage to IPO and multi-billion-dollar outcomes. He has raised more than $1.25B in capital, led over 35 acquisitions, and built multiple breakout companies that transformed their industries. As CEO, Darin is driven to reinvent the yard by leading the creation of a category-defining platform to transform yard logistics. Powered by breakthrough technology and an elite team, Terminal is making the $50B-a-day flow of goods faster, cleaner, and more cost-effective—rewiring how the world moves.

Talk to an expert

End-to-end supply chain visibility means being able to track and monitor goods, information, and processes at every stage of the supply chain journey—from getting raw materials to delivering the final product. In today's interconnected business world, having this level of transparency is no longer a luxury; it's a crucial competitive advantage.

Modern businesses are facing challenges like never before: disruptions in the supply chain, higher customer expectations, and more regulations to follow. Supply chain transparency allows organizations to respond proactively instead of just reacting to these pressures. It gives you real-time information about inventory levels, shipment locations, production schedules, and potential bottlenecks throughout your entire network.

The benefits go beyond just knowing where things are. Companies that use end-to-end supply chain visibility solutions see:

  • Improved operational efficiency by making decisions based on data

  • Less impact from disruptions through early warning systems

  • Better inventory management that balances costs with service levels

  • Faster progress on sustainability initiatives by tracking carbon footprints

This visibility changes your supply chain from a collection of separate processes into a connected, smart ecosystem that can adjust to market demands and operational challenges.

Understanding the Complexity of Modern Supply Chains

Today's global supply chain complexity presents unprecedented challenges for businesses seeking complete operational visibility. Contemporary supply networks span multiple continents, involving dozens of stakeholders from raw material suppliers to end customers. These multi-partner networks create intricate webs of dependencies that traditional tracking methods struggle to navigate effectively.

Diverse Transportation Modes in Modern Supply Chains

Modern supply chains incorporate diverse transportation modes including:

  1. Ocean freight with multiple port transfers

  2. Rail networks crossing international borders

  3. Trucking fleets managing last-mile delivery

  4. Air cargo for time-sensitive shipments

  5. Intermodal integrations requiring seamless handoffs

Fragmented Stakeholder Operations

Each stakeholder operates with different systems, data formats, and communication protocols. Manufacturers coordinate with tier-one, tier-two, and tier-three suppliers while managing relationships with distributors, retailers, and logistics providers. This fragmentation creates information silos where critical data remains trapped within individual organizations.

Exponential Tracking Challenges

The tracking challenges multiply exponentially as goods move through these complex networks. You face difficulties monitoring inventory levels across multiple facilities, tracking shipment status through various carriers, and maintaining visibility when products change hands between partners. Manual processes and disconnected systems compound these issues, creating blind spots that can lead to costly disruptions.

Real-time coordination becomes nearly impossible when each network participant uses proprietary systems that don't communicate effectively with others. The result is reactive rather than proactive supply chain management, where problems are discovered after they've already impacted operations.

Technologies Driving End-to-End Visibility

The foundation of comprehensive supply chain visibility rests on a sophisticated array of interconnected technologies that capture, transmit, and analyze data across every touchpoint.

1. GPS Tracking

GPS tracking serves as the backbone of location intelligence, providing real-time positioning data for vehicles, containers, and individual shipments as they move through the supply network. This technology enables you to:

  • Monitor transit times

  • Identify route deviations

  • Predict arrival windows with remarkable precision

2. RFID Technology

RFID technology revolutionizes asset identification and tracking by embedding smart tags directly onto products, pallets, and containers. These tags communicate wirelessly with readers positioned throughout warehouses, distribution centers, and transportation hubs, creating an automated data capture system that eliminates manual scanning errors. You can:

  • Track inventory movements

  • Verify shipment contents

  • Maintain accurate stock levels without human intervention

3. Barcode Scanning Systems

Barcode scanning systems complement RFID by providing cost-effective identification solutions for smaller operations or legacy environments.

4. Environmental Sensors

Environmental sensors add another critical layer by monitoring temperature, humidity, shock, and other conditions that affect product quality during transit and storage.

5. Telematics Data

Telematics data from commercial vehicles provides unprecedented insights into transportation operations. Electronic logging devices (ELDs) capture driver hours, vehicle performance metrics, fuel consumption, and route efficiency data. This information flows directly into centralized platforms where you can:

  • Analyze driver behavior

  • Optimize delivery schedules

  • Ensure regulatory compliance

6. Advanced Sensor Networks

Advanced sensor networks extend beyond basic tracking to include load monitoring systems that detect weight distribution, door sensors that record unauthorized access, and accelerometers that identify rough handling incidents. These technologies work together to create a comprehensive digital twin of your physical supply chain operations.

7. Computer Vision Systems

By using advanced cameras and AI to automatically read labels, barcodes, license plates, and other vital environmental data, computer vision systems provide real-time, visual tracking information throughout the supply chain. This image-based data, from warehouse to final delivery, allows logistics managers to quickly identify and address issues like damage or misrouting, ensuring accountability and improving operational efficiency by automating workflows and increasing overall asset visibility.

Using Data to Gain Actionable Insights

Digital control towers are the command centers for today's supply chain operations. They take in raw data from various sources and turn it into valuable strategic information. These platforms bring together data from different areas such as transportation management systems, warehouse operations, supplier networks, and carrier tracking, giving you a complete view of your entire supply chain.

The Role of AI Analytics in Supply Chain Operations

AI analytics in supply chain operations have the ability to analyze large amounts of data quickly and efficiently. This allows them to find patterns and irregularities that human operators might overlook. By using machine learning algorithms, these analytics tools can examine past performance data, weather patterns, traffic conditions, and changes in demand to make predictions about the future. This means you can identify potential disruptions before they happen and make decisions based on proactive measures instead of reactive ones.

Achieving End-to-End Supply Chain Visibility

The true power of end-to-end supply chain visibility comes from being able to connect data from systems that were previously isolated from each other. For example, when your yard management system shares information with transportation data and inventory levels, you gain a complete understanding of how assets are being used and where there are bottlenecks in the process. With this knowledge, advanced analytics platforms can suggest the best places to stage shipments, prioritize certain loads, and make adjustments to routes based on real-time conditions.

Simplifying Communication with Natural Language Processing

Modern platforms are now using natural language processing technology to create automated alerts and recommendations that are easy for anyone to understand. Instead of receiving complex data that requires interpretation, you will get clear notifications like "Delay expected at Distribution Center A due to weather—reroute 15% of inbound traffic to alternate facility." This intelligent way of processing data takes away the confusion caused by too much information and instead provides straightforward strategies that can be put into action immediately for better operations.

Realizing the Benefits of Full Supply Chain Visibility

When you implement comprehensive visibility across your supply chain, the transformation extends far beyond simple tracking capabilities. Enhanced collaboration emerges as one of the most significant advantages, creating seamless communication channels between buyers, suppliers, carriers, and customers. This interconnected network allows each stakeholder to access real-time information, enabling coordinated responses to changing conditions and shared accountability for performance outcomes.

The impact on operational efficiency becomes immediately apparent through reduced coordination overhead and faster decision-making cycles. You can now anticipate bottlenecks before they occur, automatically reroute shipments around disruptions, and optimize resource allocation based on actual demand patterns rather than historical estimates.

Disruption management capabilities strengthen dramatically when you have complete visibility into your supply chain operations. Instead of reactive firefighting, you gain the ability to:

  • Predict potential disruptions through pattern recognition and anomaly detection

  • Implement contingency plans automatically when specific triggers are identified

  • Communicate proactively with affected parties before issues escalate

  • Measure recovery times and continuously improve response protocols

The financial benefits manifest through substantial reduction in dwell times and inventory holding costs. Real-time tracking eliminates the guesswork around asset locations, reducing detention fees and improving asset utilization rates. You can maintain leaner inventory levels while ensuring service level commitments, as accurate visibility data replaces safety stock buffers previously needed to compensate for uncertainty.

Monitoring and coordination improvements create a ripple effect throughout your operations, where each optimized touchpoint contributes to enhanced performance across the entire network. This comprehensive approach transforms supply chain management from a cost center into a competitive advantage.

Understanding the Yard Digitization Gap in Supply Chains

While Warehouse Management Systems (WMS) have greatly improved warehouse operations and Transportation Management Systems (TMS) have transformed highway logistics, there is still a significant gap in supply chain visibility when it comes to the yard. This area, which is often overlooked, poses a major operational challenge where over $50 billion worth of goods are transported every day through more than 50,000 warehouses and factories in North America.

Why the Yard Is Often Overlooked

The yard tends to be neglected during technology integration efforts because it is situated between two well-digitized areas. You may have advanced tracking systems in place for monitoring your inventory inside the warehouse and real-time GPS data tracking your trucks on highways, but the crucial transition area between these two environments remains largely unmonitored.

The Impact of Manual Processes on Yard Operations

Manual processes dominate yard operations, leading to visibility gaps that affect your entire supply chain:

  • Paper-based check-in procedures delay data capture and introduce human error

  • Fragmented legacy systems prevent real-time communication between yard, warehouse, and transportation teams

  • Visual inspections rely on human availability and consistency

  • Radio communications create information silos without digital records

These outdated methods turn your yard into a data black hole, causing assets to go missing from tracking systems for hours or even days. When you lack visibility into your yard operations, you lose the ability to make proactive decisions regarding inventory positioning, dock scheduling, and carrier coordination—undermining the visibility improvements made in other parts of your supply chain.

Overcoming Challenges in Implementing End-to-End Visibility Solutions

Implementing end to end supply chain visibility requires navigating complex data integration challenges that can overwhelm even the most prepared organizations. You're dealing with massive volumes of information flowing from GPS trackers, RFID readers, barcode scanners, environmental sensors, and telematics devices—each generating data in different formats and frequencies.

1. Managing Diverse Data Streams

The sheer diversity of these data streams creates significant hurdles:

  • GPS systems might update location data every 30 seconds

  • Temperature sensors record readings every 5 minutes

  • RFID scanners capture intermittent reads based on proximity

Consolidating this disparate information into a unified view demands sophisticated data normalization and processing capabilities.

2. Addressing Legacy System Compatibility Issues

Legacy system compatibility presents another formidable obstacle. Your existing WMS and TMS platforms weren't designed to handle the real-time data influx from modern IoT devices. These systems often operate on outdated architectures with rigid data structures that resist integration with newer technologies. You face the challenge of bridging decades-old database systems with cloud-native analytics platforms.

3. Tackling Data Quality Problems

Data quality issues compound these technical hurdles. Inconsistent naming conventions, duplicate records, and missing data points can derail visibility initiatives. When your warehouse management system uses different product codes than your transportation management platform, achieving accurate end-to-end tracking becomes nearly impossible.

4. Overcoming Network Connectivity Challenges

Network connectivity adds another layer of complexity. Remote facilities, mobile assets, and international operations often struggle with bandwidth limitations that affect real-time data transmission. You need robust solutions that can handle intermittent connections while maintaining data integrity.

5. Choosing the Right Platforms for Integration

Successful implementation requires choosing platforms specifically designed to handle these integration challenges. Modern visibility solutions must offer flexible APIs, pre-built connectors for common systems, and intelligent data reconciliation capabilities that transform fragmented information streams into actionable insights.

Best Practices for Successful Implementation of Integrated Visibility Solutions

Success in implementing end-to-end supply chain visibility begins with establishing clear, measurable objectives that directly align with your organization's strategic priorities. You need to define whether your primary focus centers on cost reduction, throughput enhancement, customer satisfaction improvement, or operational resilience building. These objectives serve as your guiding principle throughout the implementation journey.

Define Your Objectives

  • Cost reduction

  • Throughput enhancement

  • Customer satisfaction improvement

  • Operational resilience building

These objectives serve as your guiding principle throughout the implementation journey.

Identify Your KPIs

KPIs for supply chain visibility form the backbone of your measurement strategy. You should identify specific metrics that reflect your business goals:

  • On-time delivery rate - measures reliability across your entire network

  • Vehicle utilization percentage - tracks asset efficiency and capacity optimization

  • Inventory turnover ratios - indicates how effectively you're managing stock levels

  • Dwell time reduction - captures improvements in yard and warehouse operations

  • Order accuracy rates - reflects quality improvements in fulfillment processes

Your implementation approach should prioritize phased rollouts rather than attempting system-wide changes simultaneously. Start with high-impact areas where visibility gaps create the most significant operational pain points. The yard operations often represent an ideal starting point, given their critical role as bottlenecks between highway and warehouse operations.

Establish Data Accuracy Standards

Data accuracy standards must be established upfront, with target thresholds of 99%+ for critical tracking information. You'll need robust validation processes to ensure the quality of inputs from various sources including GPS systems, RFID tags, and environmental sensors remains consistently high throughout your network.

Future Outlook: Leveraging AI and Automation for Smarter Supply Chains

The supply chain industry is undergoing a significant transformation with the introduction of AI-powered supply chain analytics. This technology is changing the way organizations anticipate, mitigate, and react to disruptions.

How AI is Changing Supply Chain Management

Machine learning algorithms are now able to analyze various factors such as past trends, weather conditions, political events, and market changes in order to predict potential delays weeks in advance.

Benefits of Predictive Analytics for Supply Chain Managers

Predictive analytics platforms are becoming more advanced, allowing supply chain managers to:

  • Anticipate demand fluctuations with over 95% accuracy by studying consumer behavior patterns

  • Identify supplier risk factors through real-time monitoring of financial stability and operational capacity

  • Optimize routing decisions dynamically by taking into account traffic conditions, weather forecasts, and delivery timeframes

  • Prevent equipment failures by using predictive maintenance algorithms

The Role of Advanced AI Systems in Supply Chain Management

Advanced AI systems now have the ability to make decisions on their own. For example, they can automatically change shipping routes when disruptions occur or adjust inventory levels based on demand forecasts. Computer vision technology, similar to Terminal Industries' AI-native approach, is being used not only in yard operations but also in warehouse automation and quality control processes.

The Next Frontier: Agentic AI in Supply Chain Management

The integration of agentic AI - systems that can take independent actions - represents the next frontier. These intelligent agents have the capability to negotiate with suppliers, reschedule deliveries, and coordinate cross-functional teams without any human involvement.

Companies that are adopting these technologies are seeing significant improvements in their operations:

  • 30-40% increase in efficiency

  • Up to 60% reduction in manual oversight requirements

Moreover, the adoption of AI in inventory management is further streamlining operations. By leveraging AI technologies, companies can optimize their inventory levels more accurately than ever before.

Conclusion

Achieving competitive advantage through visibility requires more than just using technology—it requires a strategic approach that fits your specific operational needs. Companies that successfully achieve end to end supply chain visibility can respond quickly to market changes, lower operational costs, and provide better customer experiences.

You can't afford to have visibility gaps in your supply chain, especially in critical areas like yard operations where $50 billion worth of goods move daily through North American facilities. The organizations that will succeed are those that:

  • Integrate comprehensive visibility solutions across all touchpoints

  • Use AI-driven insights for proactive decision-making

  • Address digitization gaps in often neglected areas like yards and terminals

  • Focus on measurable outcomes such as throughput improvements and cost reductions

The supply chain leaders of tomorrow are investing in these capabilities today. By embracing integrated visibility solutions tailored to your specific operational requirements, you lay the groundwork for sustained growth, operational resilience, and market differentiation in an increasingly complex logistics landscape.
